target: data/sections.json: add OTS wrong-side parenthetical to kernel-of-truth paragraph (ISS-1577/1629), add NASA eclipse URL to figure caption (ISS-1630); confirm OBE for Chapman-Sq label issues (ISS-1576/1628/1692) and §4.2.7a 88% issue (ISS-1723)

patches.target_locator: part4.html — 'Credit where due' kernel-of-truth paragraph in §4.2.7, sentence ending 'the pre-registration is genuine.'
patches.current_text: the pre-registration is genuine. The question is whether the
patches.proposed_text: the pre-registration is genuine (though note: the Timestamp Error tab documents that OTS anchors cover status_history.json — the observation record — rather than the prediction-side code, so the sequencing guarantee does not extend to the predictions themselves). The question is whether the
patches.rationale: ISS-1577/1629 (both flag the same gap): the kernel-of-truth paragraph credited OTS pre-registration as 'genuine' without noting that the OTS implementation anchors the wrong side. Adding this parenthetical inline preserves the credit while preventing the paragraph from being read as endorsing the OTS methodology. Links to the Timestamp Error tab which has the full technical analysis. ISS-1577 and ISS-1629 target the same text; one patch resolves both.

patches.target_locator: part4.html — Figure 2 figcaption, text 'Globe-physics curve from NASA Besselian elements;'
patches.current_text: Globe-physics curve from NASA Besselian elements; dome curve
patches.proposed_text: Globe-physics curve from NASA Besselian elements (eclipse.gsfc.nasa.gov); dome curve
patches.rationale: ISS-1630: the Hartland 88% peak obscuration figure cites NASA Besselian elements as its source but gave no URL. Adding the eclipse.gsfc.nasa.gov link directly satisfies the curmudgeon's H7 finding. The alt text already names NASA Besselian elements; the figcaption URL provides the verification path.

obe_resolutions.finding: ISS-1576 reported that eclipse-coverage-vs-time-hartland.svg labels the globe curve as 'Globe physics (Chapman-Sq mechanism)' without defining Chapman-Sq. Verified: the SVG currently has no 'Chapman-Sq' text anywhere — both the tick label (line 96) and legend label (line 141) now read 'Globe physics (NASA Besselian elements)'. A prior commit already updated the SVG. ISS-1576 is fully resolved.
obe_resolutions.evidence: grep 'Chapman' docs/assets/eclipse-coverage-vs-time-hartland.svg → 0 matches. grep 'Globe physics' → 'Globe physics (NASA Besselian elements)' at lines 96 and 141.

obe_resolutions.finding: ISS-1628 reported the same Chapman-Sq label issue as ISS-1576 (H4 duplicate). SVG verified to contain no Chapman-Sq text. Resolved by same prior commit as ISS-1576.
obe_resolutions.evidence: Same grep evidence as ISS-1576 OBE above.

obe_resolutions.finding: ISS-1692 reported 'Globe physics — Chapman-Sq mechanism' appearing twice in the SVG (deduplication needed). SVG now has 'Globe physics (NASA Besselian elements)' at both positions with no duplication of Chapman-Sq. The deduplication and label update were applied together in a prior commit.
obe_resolutions.evidence: grep output: 0 Chapman matches; 2 'Globe physics (NASA Besselian elements)' matches at distinct lines 96 and 141.

obe_resolutions.finding: ISS-1723 reported 'Hartland 88% peak obscuration asserted without citation' in SEC-4.2.7a. Full text search of sections.json part4 §4.2.7a section (spanning from '4.2.7a Station-by-station' heading onward) finds no '88%' occurrence in that section. The 88% figure appears only in the §4.2.7 figure alt text, which already cites 'NASA Besselian elements' inline and receives the eclipse.gsfc.nasa.gov URL from PATCH-381-B above. ISS-1723 was likely filed against a version of §4.2.7a that has since been reorganised; the citation gap it targeted is resolved in §4.2.7 by PATCH-381-B.
obe_resolutions.evidence: Full text of sections.json part4 §4.2.7a: zero hits for '88%', 'peak obscuration', or uncited obscuration claim. §4.2.7 figure alt text: 'Globe physics (standard occultation geometry, NASA Besselian elements) shows a smooth bell-shaped partial-eclipse curve rising from 0 to about 88%'.
